posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked:
Sorting through some little-known under-priced wines this morning I realized that I got the tip off about these wines from a few wine critics. While occasionally I use critic’s scores as a sign that inflation is soon to come on a given wine, and other times I use a critic to get an idea of what shifts and changes are happening at a given winery (an increase of cab franc in a blend, shifting from barrique to tonneaux). I most frequently use reviews to see how much air a wine might need, or if it should be consumed soon (usually CT).

I am sure there are several other reasons I read critic’s reviews, but these are what came to mind on a first reflection.

I am curious to what others look for in a reviewer/ critic. Feel free to be more direct about the reviewer as well, such informative, unbiased, curious…

posts.name: Peter Panzica
posts.cooked:
I look for consistency across reviewers whose palates I feel align with mine.
I cross reference what I see with CT as well.
I look at the enthusiasm that comes across in the note.
I look at aging windows as I already have to much wine and want something built to last.
There is one reviewer I disregard.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
something that we’ll never see - except occasionally from Gilman; that the reviewer really doesn’t like the wine at all. some do a better job than others at giving a big score and in the review itself intimate that the style isn’t their preferred one, but that’s it. in their efforts to make reviews as democratic and egalitarian as possible, they’ve made their reviews less useful (or at least the score - so maybe that’s a good thing).

the reality is that no one wants to pay for low reviews of wines they may have purchased.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
For many, it starts with a lack of confidence in this huge landscape of wine, and a lack of confidence in themselves and their palate to navigate it. That’s where a confident critic steps into the opportunity and confidently instructs them “what is good and what is not”. The critic may well influence people to appreciate what they initially don’t like, or even start to doubt their enjoyment of something they do like. People are drawn to this confidence, as they feel ignorant and confused. Confidence is the prime requirement and a score as a number gives ‘certainty’.

So let’s take it a few months, years, even decades on, to someone who has now grown their experience quite a bit, but their confidence isn’t quite at the point where they’re prepared to let go of the safety of being told what is good and what is not.

At this point they’ll have regions they know better, and some producers they may know well. If the critic also likes the wines they themselves enjoy, they might be encouraged to try/buy the wines that critic also likes from the region. For regions they don’t know, they may initially accept the disliking a wine the critic had lauded, or finding a wine they love got trashed by a critic. These can at first be waived away as bottle variation, and a clever critic keeps a nice tight range of scores so these situations stand out less. For this, consistency of style preference can help, and a reader may remain happy following a critic forever, because there are few ‘WTF’ moments, whilst one who consistently differs from the reader will eventually see the relationship end.

At some point the person might notice that the advice they used to lean so heavily on, was not so useful, and could even be counter-productive, ending up with old bottles in the cellar they no longer want to drink. Maybe their tasting opportunities allow them to build up confidence to the level, that they simply follow what their palate tells them, no longer needing the instructions of others.

Are critics still useful then? IMO not really, but a good ‘wine writer’ can fill the gap, looking to be more informative than instructional. Someone who shares deeper insight, but without trying to impose their wine preferences on the reader. Being able to describe how Brunate vineyard differs to Rochette and how this might influence the wines, or the differences between Rolly-Gassman and Trimbach holdings, viticulture & viniculture and how that might influence the wines.

Most wine writers still can’t let go of the need to ‘recommend’ (and probably also true of most readers), but trust should start to change at this point, from blind trust in telling the reader what wines to buy, to trust in their experience to inform the reader of aspects that may differ and where the reader’s own preferences might take them.

Throughout this, I do still place some value in tasting notes, albeit CT has been a great eye-opener to the extreme variations that exist, and there is wide variety in ability to convey what’s in the glass, and a few too many who follow the critic’s lead and think the primary aspect to convey is how much they liked it.

Nothing ever is as useful as having a glass of the wine in front of you though.

posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked:
Just about no one.

The 2003 San Giusto a Rentennano Percarlo was given an 88 by Galloni back at WA. IMO this has outmatched many 97+ point wines from Galloni, Larner, Parker… And those are critics I trust.

Lower the ratings, better the price for me!

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
since i mentioned him, it’s worth reading about Gilman’s Roadkill

https://www.wineberserkers.com/t/roadkill-highly-rated-wines-of-dubious-quality-by-john-gilman/

perhaps the greatest wine experience of my life was sitting next to him at a dinner here in manhattan when he tried his first Sine Qua Non syrah. I imagine the emotions as similar to what those scientists felt when the first atomic bomb was detonated.

edit: wow that old thread is terrific. gilman pops in as do other wine luminaries of times past. worth a good scan for sure. thanks to @ToddFrench for preserving these time capsules.

link_counts.title: Roadkill: Highly Rated Wines of Dubious Quality, by John Gilman

posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked:
Ian_Sutton:

These can at first be waived away as bottle variation, and a clever critic keeps a nice tight range of scores so these situations stand out less.

Interestingly I find the average score on CT to be even more narrow.

Ian_Sutton:

Nothing ever is as useful as having a glass of the wine in front of you though.

Not much that I could agree with more than this!

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Ian_Sutton:

At this point they’ll have regions they know better, and some producers they may know well. If the critic also likes the wines they themselves enjoy, they might be encouraged to try/buy the wines that critic also likes from the region. For regions they don’t know, they may initially accept the disliking a wine the critic had lauded, or finding a wine they love got trashed by a critic.

I think this is a valuable thing as far as mainstream wine magazines. Some number of readers are going to be willing to take the plunge into buying their first Chinon, Etna Rosso, New Zealand pinot, Bandol, etc. because they saw a good review and score of a specific bottle in Wine Spectator, where they may have never gone and discovered that category otherwise.

And notwithstanding all the critic hate around here, the odds are, the one that got a high score will be a good wine, or at least a good first introduction to the category, and then they may eventually do what you describe and explore it more and rely less and less on reviews and scores.

posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked:
I agree. The only issue is that the reason it is the first wine they buy from those regions is because of the critic acclaim elsewhere.

I think that the more reviewers, and the less reviews per reviewer, the better off we are. In some sense, that is what we get with CT and WB.

posts.name: Matt K1llman
posts.cooked:
A lot of good things to look for in here, I would say a top factor for me is that they occasionally come across wines they don’t like very much and are honest and open about that.

As a secondary to that I enjoy when they determine WHY they don’t like it, whether it’s personal preference, disjointed wine, or flaws. I find Stephen McConnell does a good job of this, although he can be otherwise polarizing.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: the ‘why’ is always more useful/interesting

posts.name: K@m@@l H@que
posts.cooked: I look for a lack of a scarf.

posts.name: Andrew L.
posts.cooked: This is great – does anyone happen to have a list of the wines? The linked PDF in the post appears to be gone.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
K_N_Haque:

I look for a lack of a scarf.

and/or a villa in tuscany

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
I think it is fair use to post this, if not I apologize to John in advance and will delete. John is my favorite wine writer because of his candor in addition to his great palate. Without further ado, my favorite tasting notes of all time:

  • 2005 Mollydooker Enchanted Path - Australia, South Australia, Fleurieu, McLaren Vale
    This heady wine weighs in at a cool sixteen percent alcohol, and is one of the most repulsive wines that I have tasted in twenty-five years as a professional taster. The wild and offensive nose offers up notes of marinated raspberries, chocolate liqueur, toasted coconut and a lumberyard full of raw, sawdusty oak. On the palate the wine is full-bodied, fat, cloying, syrupy and excessively scarred by its high alcohol, with a thick and utterly repulsive finish, that alas, lasts much longer than is healthy to the taster. The ’05 Mollydooker “Enchanted Path” is the vinous equivalent of toxic waste. (47 pts.)
  • 2005 Mollydooker Shiraz Carnival of Love - Australia, South Australia, Fleurieu, McLaren Vale
    This lovely little gem from Mollydooker comes in an even more high octane alcohol level of 16.5%, and is even strikingly more offensive than the wine above. The sickly sweet nose (I wonder what the residual sugar level is on this wine) is a blur of black raspberries, peanut butter, rotting beef, raw oak and chocolate. On the palate the wine is full-bodied, fat and oily, with an almost macabre ugliness to it, no focus, no structure and an aggressively hot, bitter and dry woody finish. I could not spit this nasty wine out fast enough. (45 pts.)
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Brandon Econ
posts.cooked: Someone that confirms my biases.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked:
timeliness

new producers

changes at domains - positively or negatively

vintage assessments

any major outliers for example a consistent 90 point wine that is somehow a much better wine in a particular vintage

Don’t really care about a zillion tasting notes with descriptors.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
thanks for that. there are 270 (!!) notes on CT for the Carnivore of Love and they are overwhelmingly positive / great. There are handful of awful reviews, but the attendant scores are still 85-88 which mind boggles me.

I think there’s a very strong argument for requiring reviewers to provide this service to their readers. A control if you will. For sure, if you enjoy these types of wines, you should not pay Gilman to tell you what to drink. And vice versa?

reply_to_user.name: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.cooked:
What do you look for in a reviewer/critic or a review?

At this point, nothing really, at least as far as reviews go. I don’t read any with regularity and have no subscriptions to any magazines any more, as they were just accumulating without ever having been read.

But, like anything else, if someone writes well and has something interesting to say about a subject, I’m happy to read it. Scores and TNs are really of no interest, but if someone were to do a profile of a region, a wine, a person, whatever, and it’s written well, I find those very enjoyable. And there are a few people I know or have met who, were they to offer a positive recommendation, I would consider. But I’m really trying to buy very little these days, and my B-Day purchases were all things that seemed interesting that I knew little about.

It’s understandable that some people do have faves though. In my earlier days, I paid more attention and read just about any wine publication I could find.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
M.Kaplan:

I think it is fair use to post this, if not I apologize to John in advance and will delete. John is my favorite wine writer because of his candor in addition to his great palate. Without further ado, my favorite tasting notes of all time:

  • 2005 Mollydooker Enchanted Path - Australia, South Australia, Fleurieu, McLaren Vale
    This heady wine weighs in at a cool sixteen percent alcohol, and is one of the most repulsive wines that I have tasted in twenty-five years as a professional taster. The wild and offensive nose offers up notes of marinated raspberries, chocolate liqueur, toasted coconut and a lumberyard full of raw, sawdusty oak. On the palate the wine is full-bodied, fat, cloying, syrupy and excessively scarred by its high alcohol, with a thick and utterly repulsive finish, that alas, lasts much longer than is healthy to the taster. The ’05 Mollydooker “Enchanted Path” is the vinous equivalent of toxic waste. (47 pts.)
  • 2005 Mollydooker Shiraz Carnival of Love - Australia, South Australia, Fleurieu, McLaren Vale
    This lovely little gem from Mollydooker comes in an even more high octane alcohol level of 16.5%, and is even strikingly more offensive than the wine above. The sickly sweet nose (I wonder what the residual sugar level is on this wine) is a blur of black raspberries, peanut butter, rotting beef, raw oak and chocolate. On the palate the wine is full-bodied, fat and oily, with an almost macabre ugliness to it, no focus, no structure and an aggressively hot, bitter and dry woody finish. I could not spit this nasty wine out fast enough. (45 pts.)

And therein is why he really is one of the best critics. Who else does this?

PS. WK did come down on Rolland, so perhaps we have 2 critics in total that speak truth.

Anyone else?

reply_to_user.name: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked: I take additional joy from these tasting notes because my brother loves the wines. Loves. So much so that he had me reach out to a friend in Adelaide, who arranged a visit and tasting with Sparky 15 years ago. These very wines were among those Sparky poured for my brother.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

PS. WK did come down on Rolland, so perhaps we have 2 critics in total that speak truth.

Many might love the Rolland style. “Truth” is probably the wrong word in this context. (Btw, I don’t like the Rolland style… but I have friends that love Pavie or Peby Faugeres).

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
What I would love to see more in critics notes is context. That would be most helpful but beside people on CT, this is almost never done.

I don’t get why critics won’t reference one wine to other wines in other vintages, compare it to the same wine in other vintages. That would be more helpful than all of those descriptors they rely on. Look at Will’s comment on the 2020 Figeac below. That information will be more helpful than all the critics tasting notes on this wine that are/will be published.

2019 bordeaux

2016 is super classic, but quite youthfully shut down and will demand some patience. 2020 for me is more typically Figeac than 2019: it’s very deep and incipiently complex, a touch less extrovert than 19 without being austere, and its oak is more completely integrated. 2016, 2019 and 2020 are the three best vintages of the Faye era so far, but 2020 is my favorite.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Andy_Sc:

What I would love to see more in critics notes is context. That would be most helpful but beside people on CT, this is almost never done.

I don’t get why critics won’t reference one wine to other wines in other vintages, compare it to the same wine in other vintages. That would be more helpful than all of those descriptors they rely on. Look at Will’s comment on the 2020 Figeac below. That information will be more helpful than all the critics tasting notes on this wine that are/will be published.

2019 bordeaux

2016 is super classic, but quite youthfully shut down and will demand some patience. 2020 for me is more typically Figeac than 2019: it’s very deep and incipiently complex, a touch less extrovert than 19 without being austere, and its oak is more completely integrated. 2016, 2019 and 2020 are the three best vintages of the Faye era so far, but 2020 is my favorite.

I couldn’t agree more. In my own way, I try to do this, but with neither the ability nor the range of experience of a wine critic. When I read a critic’s work, I look for context, so comparing with other vintages, but also with other wines, because these are reference points which speak to many people. Also, I look for style references. I used to be a big fan of Neal Martin and still enjoy his work, but I’ve become increasingly frustrated by his notes as they do not tell me enough about the style of the wine.

I had a minor example this week with a Brillette (Moulis) 2016 - Neal described the “crisp tannin” and “fine acidity”, plus the “fresh and tensile finish”, all of which prompted me to buy it, but he did not mention the lush, voluptuous fruit which sends it dangerously towards spoofy land. So for me, job not done.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
True.

It was poorly stated. It was more meant in the sense of calling something bad if you think it is bad.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Agreed, context and comparatives are really a big deal for me. Understanding how a particular wine performed relative to other vintages of the same wine, is remarkably helpful.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:
M.Kaplan:

I think it is fair use to post this, if not I apologize to John in advance and will delete. John is my favorite wine writer because of his candor in addition to his great palate. Without further ado, my favorite tasting notes of all time:

  • 2005 Mollydooker Enchanted Path - Australia, South Australia, Fleurieu, McLaren Vale
    This heady wine weighs in at a cool sixteen percent alcohol, and is one of the most repulsive wines that I have tasted in twenty-five years as a professional taster. The wild and offensive nose offers up notes of marinated raspberries, chocolate liqueur, toasted coconut and a lumberyard full of raw, sawdusty oak. On the palate the wine is full-bodied, fat, cloying, syrupy and excessively scarred by its high alcohol, with a thick and utterly repulsive finish, that alas, lasts much longer than is healthy to the taster. The ’05 Mollydooker “Enchanted Path” is the vinous equivalent of toxic waste. (47 pts.)
  • 2005 Mollydooker Shiraz Carnival of Love - Australia, South Australia, Fleurieu, McLaren Vale
    This lovely little gem from Mollydooker comes in an even more high octane alcohol level of 16.5%, and is even strikingly more offensive than the wine above. The sickly sweet nose (I wonder what the residual sugar level is on this wine) is a blur of black raspberries, peanut butter, rotting beef, raw oak and chocolate. On the palate the wine is full-bodied, fat and oily, with an almost macabre ugliness to it, no focus, no structure and an aggressively hot, bitter and dry woody finish. I could not spit this nasty wine out fast enough. (45 pts.)

And therein is why he really is one of the best critics. Who else does this?

FWIW I don’t rate either of those tasting notes, as it’s clear he hates the wines and wants to hurl abuse at them. Nowhere is there any thought to why someone might like them / who they might appeal to.

The over-riding feeling, is he’s sneering at the wines (and those that like them).

I used to berate Parker for doing similar, but this is every bit as one-eyed as Parker was.

For context, this is very much not my style of wine either.

posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked: I agree. It seems as misleading as the critics who give so many wines 100pts. But I can see why many would like these types of reviews, because we do live in a world where stores are slamming the highest scores at us and misguiding people who are not quite as knowledgeable about wine.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
I obviously disagree. Certain wines and winemakers and critics are deserving of scorn and opprobrium.

Do you prefer this note?
Deep garnet-brick colored, the 2005 Carnival of Love displays a more earthy, meaty, gamey character with plenty of coffee and damp loam aromas fused with smoked bacon, some iron ore, Chinese plums, a little floral note and even a bit of truffles character coming through. It is very rich and full-bodied, with the acid line standing out a little from the very concentrated and quite evolved fruit core. Still, it has amazing layers and a lot to give on the very long, licorice-laced finish, even if just a bit warm. Drink it to 2020+.

Or this one?
Amazingly, the 2005 Carnival of Love ratchets up the level of quality. A selection of the finest Shiraz barrels, it boasts an extraordinary black/purple color, gorgeous notes of blackberry liqueur, camphor, and smoke, and an enormous voluptuousness that would make even Pamela Anderson jealous. Great purity, symmetry, and harmony as well as remarkable balance for such a rich, intense wine, as it sat in the glass espresso, vanillin, white chocolate, and additional black fruits also emerged. It should drink well for a decade, although the winemakers suggest it be consumed over the next four years. If you are wondering what to serve with this beauty, Sarah and Sparky Marquis recommend such fish as marlin, hammerhead, and great white shark, white meats such as snake and crocodile, red meats such as emu and kangaroo, and aged wallaby mild cheese. Yum!

“Rated” 98 and 99 points, respectively, by Lisa Perotti-Brown and Robert Parker. And 45 points by Gilman.

I am happy that there are wine writers like John Gilman who push back, hard, on this sort of hackery and wine making.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
insights and background I don’t have.
Avoidance of hyperbole and flowery adjectives
Parallel my taste—that I like wines they like, dislike ones they dislike
Timeliness in reporting
Expanding my boundaries of knowledge, pretty much my first point.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Hoping to bring more and more of this to our Bordeaux coverage (where I venture it is especially useful). It’s quite a common observation from subscribers.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Good thread topic. I’ve been at this wine obsession for over a decade and still read quite a few critics. Like many I look for overall discussion of producers’ vineyard and wine making practices, changes in same, or generational changes, and history of vineyards and families. Context for how the wines might taste differently or similarly to other producers or vintages. I’d love to see more discussions of style, but wine writers really shy away from that it seems.

Certainly reviewer consistency and clarity of alignment with my palate count for a lot.

Still, I do read individual wine reviews. I don’t really care which variety of pomegranate is reflected in the nose of a wine, but I am interested in where value lies, or which wines seem to be indicative of how changing climate is affecting vineyard quality, or qualities. Comparisons across producers and vintages are really helpful.

If I could taste broadly the regions I love, every vintage, before making purchase decisions, then I would need critics less. But even before Covid this was impossible. Now: still impossible. The critics are able to taste unbelievably deeply and widely, helping me find new producers in regions I love (e.g. H. Magnien in Burgundy) or helping me explore new to me areas (e.g. Northern Rhône the past few years).

When I look to make a decision on a wine/producer that I don’t have much experience with I look for reviews, search CT and here on WB, and use all that info. Sometimes the only info I can find is on Vinous or in Burghound’s database. Sometimes there’s a ton of discussion here over many years. All useful.

IMHO for Berserkers who know what they like and buy the same 5, 10, or 30 producers every vintage, I doubt critics/reviewers are very useful. I like exploring new regions and producers all the time. I consume whatever sources of information I can find as inputs to my exploration.

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
In general there’s 2 real things I look for in a critic:

  1. To highlight an exceptional vintage or let me know to be wary of a rough vintage.

  2. To introduce me to wines I wouldn’t ordinarily come across to get good wine at lower prices. I already know that Leflaive, Chave, Harlan … whatever … is excellent. Whether Krug is a 96 or 98 in their subjective opinion isn’t terribly important to me. What I don’t know - and can’t possibly do - is taste hundreds or thousands of wines available at more affordable prices and pick standout QPRs.

I’d prefer if reviewers were more transparent about winemaking style sometimes, but as long as it’s easy enough to read between the lines, I’m happy. While it would get tiresome to read that in every review of every cuvée, it would be nice in the digital age, if each review included links to producer profiles that had this info more readily accessible.

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
I look for the following in tasting notes:

  1. Like Justin mentions above, I want to know in which style the wine was made. Modern/ripe vs Old school/leaner/lower alcohol. I know this is a minefield of stereotypes and trigger-words across a broad spectrum, but give me a general idea what zip-code the wine inhabits.
  2. My favorite wines are Cabernet based blends, so I want to know what the blends are. Ex: The wine is labeled as Cabernet Sauv, but there’s 5%Cab franc in the blend. The much maligned Wine Advocate has always been a leader in this area, IMO.
  3. I love Cabs/Cab blends containing a red fruit profile so I pay more attention to reviews or reviewers delineating this. Galloni consistently does this in a way that matches my impressions.
  4. I prefer reviews which comment on balance & acidity (which is similar to my #1). My favorite wines have that acid/lift that makes the wine feel weightless dancing around your mouth. Galloni usually conveys this also.

posts.name: Eric Tomasi
posts.cooked: Digging this up, but I saw a Garagiste offer that I wasnt very interested in, but the scores/review was by TDB, That stuck out to me as I had no idea who that reviewer or review team it was. Is it “the Drinks Business” magazine/website? If so, who does their burgundy reviews.
